bert_text: $CMG just delivered a Q1 that resets the narrative for this name. While the tape has been punishing the stock recently, the underlying numbers suggest the business is hitting an inflection point where it matters most: actual foot traffic. The headline beat on comparable sales (0.5% vs. expectations of a 0.8% decline) is the primary signal here. In a macro environment where the consumer is clearly pinched, Chipotle is managing to flip transactions back to green. The Data Points: Transaction Growth: This wasn't just a price-hike story. Guest counts are moving in the right direction. "The increase was driven by new restaurant openings and, to a lesser extent, a 0.5% increase in comparable restaurant sales due to higher transactions of 0.6%" Revenue Performance: Total revs hit $3.1 billion, a 7.4% YoY increase, narrowly beating the $3.07B consensus. The Menu Catalyst: The return of a fan favorite is doing the heavy lifting for traffic. "The Chicken al Pastor dish that Chipotle reintroduced earlier this year has been the single biggest driver of consumer traffic at Chipotle recently." Management’s Buyback Floor: They aren't just talking about value... they're putting capital to work at levels higher than where we’ve been trading. "During the first quarter of 2026 we repurchased $700.8 million of stock at an average price per share of $36.14." Nice! The Thesis: Margins did take a hit... adjusted restaurant-level operating margin fell to 23.7% due to beef and wage inflation... but that is a well known headwind. The washed out technical setup we’ve seen lately has likely priced in much of this margin compression. With the 0.6% transaction growth and 42 new Chipotlanes added this quarter, the operational engine is far more resilient than the recent price action implied. The setup, IMO, looks phenomenal here, and I’m looking to scale in methodically for the longer-term position. Good luck to everyone.

bert_text: $CMG Opened a new long in CMG on 4/9... short the May $30 puts. Breakeven on assignment lands in the low-$29 range, which puts me right at the multi-year horizontal shelf the chart has defended repeatedly. 32 DTE, and yes, this position straddles the 4/29 earnings print -- that's part of why the premium is what it is, and I'm sized accordingly. The setup: – Weekly chart shows a clean trading channel between the ~$29.58 floor and ~$42.75 ceiling... every meaningful turn since 2022 has happened at one of those two rails – Weekly RSI mid-40s, weekly stochastics under 19... oversold on the timeframe that matters for swing structure – Down ~13% on the quarter, ~17% on the half, ~32% on the year, ~42% from the 52-week high – PT $42.75 isn't aggressive... it's a tag of the upper channel rail, the same level that capped price multiple times through 2023 and again on the 2025 retest The thesis: this was the Wall Street darling no one could get enough of until comp growth rolled, traffic softened, and the market decided the brand was broken. It isn't. Sales still grew Y/Y, operating margin is sitting at 16.8%, ROE near 47%, and management has been pretty open about refocusing on the operational fundamentals that built the brand in the first place -- throughput, food quality, and the in-store experience -- instead of chasing the next loyalty gimmick. The multiple compression has done most of the work already: forward P/E down to ~25 from the 50+ range this name used to command. Not cheap in absolute terms, but cheap for what CMG has historically been. Bear case I'm respecting: GLP-1 demand impact on QSR/fast-casual is a real structural overhang and nobody knows how it shakes out long-term. Comps could deteriorate further if the consumer keeps weakening, and a soft 4/29 print could pressure the shelf in a hurry -- that's exactly where I get assigned and start owning the name at a level I'm comfortable with. Analyst tape is genuinely mixed right now: Mizuho upgraded to Outperform with a $40 PT in March, BNP initiated Neutral $37 around the same time. Not a unanimous bull case, which I actually prefer when I'm taking the other side of the fear. Plan: collect the premium, take assignment in the low-$29S if the print disappoints, and start scaling into shares from a level the chart has defended for three-plus years. Channel bottom is where you want to be a buyer, not the middle. Just my read. DYOR before putting any money to work. Giddy up!

bert_text: Alsea has renewed its licensing agreement with Starbucks, extending its rights to develop and operate the coffee chain across around a dozen countries until 2046, reinforcing a long-term strategic partnership. Under the deal, Alsea will maintain development obligations for 2026–2028, with both companies jointly defining future expansion plans, while keeping terms broadly in line with the previous agreement. $ALSEA* Starbucks remains Alsea’s most important brand, with nearly 2,000 stores as of March 2026, making it the company’s largest format and a key revenue driver across its portfolio of restaurant and café brands. The coffee chain represents roughly one-third of Alsea’s sales, while also holding a dominant position in Mexico’s café market with close to a 47% share. The partnership, which began in 2002, now spans Latin America and parts of Europe, including markets such as Spain, France, the Netherlands, and several countries in South America. $SBUX

bert_text: Starbucks will cut another 300 corporate jobs as part of its broader cost-reduction plan, including roles in its Seattle headquarters and a review of international positions. The company is also closing smaller regional offices in cities like Atlanta, Chicago, Dallas, and Burbank, shifting most affected staff to remote work. The cuts are part of CEO Brian Niccol’s restructuring strategy aimed at simplifying operations, improving efficiency, and reducing $2B in costs over two years. Starbucks has already eliminated more than 2,000 corporate roles and expects about $400M in restructuring charges, including severance and store-related adjustments. The company is also relocating part of its corporate workforce to Nashville, where it is investing $100M and planning to eventually base around 2,000 employees, leveraging lower costs and strategic location advantages. $SBUX

bert_text: Notes from $SBUX investor day: The theme of the SBUX Investor Day was moving to offense. While there’s still work to be done in their “Back to Starbucks” turnaround, they’re ahead of schedule and exceedingly confident in their approach. This is the time to keep leaning in, and so they are. Notes from Chief Brand Officer Tressie Liberman: Starbucks is revamping the afternoon menu to drive more ritual-based behavior like it enjoys in the morning. The idea is that more frequency will follow. They’re adding ube and coconut flavors to the menu, a new highly personalizable chai concept and more matcha-based options. They’re also sharpening culinary efforts for the bake case, with a planned strawberry matcha loaf and other options. These additions follow some significant menu clutter simplification where it cut low performing items. As I’ve spoken about in the past, the old leadership team abandoned stage-gate processes for rolling out new menu items. They guessed and hoped. Shockingly, that didn’t work. All of this should work alongside an expanded Energy Refreshers push and a continued focus on protein add-ons. The protein line-up rapidly crossed $1B in annualized sales and that looks to be just the beginning. With Niccol at the helm, we don’t have to worry about exciting releases frequently swinging and missing. Once something is rolled out, the decision is already based on a large base of user feedback and data. And if something fails? Their innovation pipeline has been restocked and their ability to generate shots on goal will greatly increase going forward. In other news, SBUX is updating its loyalty program structure to include Green, Gold and Reserve tiers. This will debut on March 10th and will feature various perks depending on degree of customer loyalty. They also deepened their tight Delta partnership so customers can earn Delta loyalty points with SBUX purchases. For the morning day-part, they’re introducing the “Better Morning” guarantee. This will replace incorrect beverages with a “no questions asked policy.” SBUX will add some of the popular features like its micro-lot cold brew from its Reserve store concepts to the rest of its stores. It’s also adding the specialized bean flights in some of its highest-volume urban stores. Notes from COO Mike Grams: Grams focused on all the ways SBUX can continue to improve encouraging store throughput trends. This is such an important metric for comp store sales. When folks are on their way to work, speed, reliability and precision are everything. If you’re late on their cup… they’re late and annoyed too. And they’ll go somewhere else. This team has made great strides to ensure that doesn’t happen. They’re rolling out a tweaked Siren Craft System with enhanced order fulfillment workflows. This will build on the success of its upgraded mobile order sequencing algorithms and a new ability to pause mobile order intake if stores are overwhelmed. This version of Siren Craft eliminates the expensive, disruptive equipment installation the old team had planned and pursues better throughput via work quality and labor coverage instead. In other throughput news, they plan to introduce the Mastrena III that cuts espresso shot creation in half. For its workers, SBUX is introducing a new point-of-sale system that cuts new employee training time from 90 days to 7 and a new AI app allowing these workers to ask questions about policies or menu items if they need help. To improve menu availability rates and cut waste, they introduced a computer vision tool to automate inventory management – similar to what Cava has effectively deployed. Finally, new SBUX stores will introduce what they’re calling sound shields to buffer loud blender and other equipment noise. This is one of the most common complaints for people who consider working in one of their locations. Notes from Starbucks International CEO Brady Brewer: The change in the China model will mean about 90% of international SBUX locations are licensed. They plan to accelerate India store growth and double total locations there to reach 1,000 by 2028. They have a clear line of sight to productively doubling international store count over the long-term. Plenty of growth left. SBUX is opening an R&D hub in Milan to develop menu concepts more relevant for other markets. In exciting channel development news, they unveiled the “Starbucks Anywhere Concept.” This will let them add smaller locations in just 30 days using a standardized lego-like design. They plan to add these to cruise ships, trains etc. Reminder of the Financial Targets they Set: For review, SBUX guided to 3%+ U.S. and global comp store sales growth in each of the next three years. While store growth will remain muted near-term, they see revenue growth reaching at least 5% Y/Y by fiscal year 2028, with a 14.25% EBIT margin and $3.67 in EPS ($3.52 post China deal closure). This has driven 6% boosts to EBIT and EPS estimates for 2028 since the event. To get there, SBUX plans to ramp new store growth to 2,000/year (400/year in the USA) by 2028 and stay there for a while. It also plans to pull $3B in costs out of the supply chain and other parts of the business to find investments while driving expected operating leverage. They also maintained a 50% shareholder payout ratio target via a blend of dividends and eventually buybacks. Quick Thoughts: No change to my point of view here. SBUX is back to executing at a strong clip and Niccol is the perfect CEO to usher in its next generation of success. I see real upside potential to the targets they just set and remain a confident shareholder.

bert_text: $LKNCY $SBUX $BROS Luckin Coffee isn’t really a café chain, it’s a daily consumption platform. The app learns what customers drink, when they drink it, and where they’ll be, turning coffee from a decision into a routine. Predictable routines create predictable demand, allowing Luckin to optimize purchasing, roasting, and logistics at scale while lowering costs with every new store. At the same time, coupons, bundles, and prepaid balances mean customers often fund purchases before drinking them. Luckin frequently receives cash first and fulfills later, giving visibility into future consumption and stabilizing margins. Switching becomes harder because users hold value and rewards inside. Competitors fight. Luckin owns behavior. Each store adds data, efficiency, and float strengthening the network. This is why some investors don’t see Luckin as retail, but as consumer infrastructure: a habit-driven platform monetized through coffee, where growth compounds instead of saturates. 🚀

bert_text: Starbucks is reintroducing tiers to its North American loyalty program as part of a broader turnaround aimed at boosting customer visits and spending. Announced at the company’s investor day, the move reflects a shift back toward rewarding its most frequent customers more distinctly. The revamped Starbucks Rewards program, launching March 10, will feature three tiers—green, gold, and reserve—based on stars earned over a 12-month period. Higher tiers will offer richer benefits, including faster point accumulation, non-expiring stars, and access to exclusive merchandise and events. Core redemption options will remain largely unchanged, with some added perks such as monthly free drink modifications and a new $2 discount reward. Management said the previous single-tier approach failed to properly recognize the brand’s most loyal customers. The update follows recent earnings that showed traffic growth from both loyalty members and non-members for the first time in nearly four years. $SBUX

bert_text: Starbucks shares rose sharply after the company reported mixed quarterly results but delivered a key positive surprise: customer traffic grew for the first time in two years, signaling early progress in CEO Brian Niccol’s turnaround plan. While Starbucks missed fiscal first-quarter earnings expectations, revenue beat estimates and same-store sales increased for a second consecutive quarter. Global comparable sales rose 4%, driven by a 3% increase in transactions, marking the first traffic growth in two years. U.S. same-store sales climbed 4%, supported by strong demand for holiday offerings, while China posted 7% comparable sales growth. The company reinstated financial guidance, projecting fiscal 2026 adjusted EPS of $2.15–$2.40, slightly below Wall Street expectations, and at least 3% growth in global and U.S. same-store sales. Margins were pressured by turnaround-related costs, higher coffee prices, and tariffs. $SBUX

bert_text: $TSLA It’s surprising nobody is discussing this, but a SpaceX IPO could force structural changes to YieldMax’s TSLA‑based ETFs (TSLY & CRSH). If Musk folds Tesla into SpaceX, TSLA may stop existing as a standalone underlying. Legally, single‑stock option‑income ETFs must track a live, listed stock with listed options. (2) Liquidation – if SpaceX has no options, YieldMax must close the funds and return NAV to holders. bert_text: $MRAM $LUNR $RKLB $TSLA $NVDA NASA’s new Moon Base I partners announcement shows why MRAM could be well positioned. Blue Origin won the CLPS-LTV lunar terrain vehicle and the Blue Moon MK1 lander for sustained South Pole operations targeted around fall 2026. Everspin ($MRAM) is supplying radiation-hardened MRAM for the lander’s systems. JPL is involved through Firefly delivering their Moonfall hopping drones, with Astrolab and Lunar Outpost handling rovers. This builds on JPL’s prior use of Everspin MRAM in Mars missions like Perseverance, where it has performed in high-radiation environments. Why MRAM fits these missions so well: • It’s non-volatile: retains data even without power, critical for landers, rovers, and drones that may go dormant or face power interruptions. • Fast read/write like DRAM but with much lower power draw. • Extremely resistant to radiation-induced bit flips from cosmic rays — a big issue on the Moon and Mars where there’s no magnetic field protection. • Reliable for flight control, sensor data logging, and edge computing in harsh conditions. The same strengths translate directly to humanoid robots here on Earth. Mobile bots need compact, low-power memory for real-time AI processing that holds state without constant energy or risk of data loss in variable environments. As robot deployments scale, proven radiation-hard and rugged memory tech from space could accelerate adoption for reliable edge AI brains. Quiet validation across NASA partners and growing dual-use potential in robotics. Worth watching.

bert_text: $MRAM $MU $TSLA $SNDK Imagine a robot or humanoid that has to move around and think super fast — like a kid riding a bike. Now imagine the bike’s “brain” forgets everything the second the battery flickers even for one second. That would be dangerous, right? That’s what happens with normal computer memory (DRAM). It loses everything when power dips. MRAM from Everspin is different. It’s like a magic memory that never forgets, even if the power turns off for a second. It stays on instantly, uses almost no power when resting, and is extra tough for tough jobs. This is the missing safety piece for real Physical AI robots, humanoids, and edge devices. Without safe, reliable memory right next to the motors and sensors, the whole system can fail at the worst time. Everyone talks about batteries, motors, and chips… but almost no one is talking about this important safety memory link.
